text: Marcus Aurelius opened each morning with a deliberate act the Stoics called prosoche — a self-directed attention to the quality of one's own mind. Before engaging the world, Marcus rehearsed anticipated frictions: difficult people, interruptions, ingratitude. This was not pessimism but inoculation. By pre-loading awareness of what the day might demand, he narrowed the gap between impulse and response. Epictetus called this gap the only territory a person truly owns. Prosoche was the daily labor of guarding that territory — not once, at dawn, but continuously, returning attention to the present moment each time circumstance pulled it elsewhere.
question: According to the passage, why did Marcus Aurelius rehearse anticipated difficulties each morning?

options.text: To cultivate pessimism and lower his expectations for the day
To narrow the gap between impulse and response through prior awareness
To satisfy Epictetus's requirement of a morning ritual
To identify enemies and prepare arguments against them
